full_text:
Written on: 26.05.2026

By: María Hincapié-Otero

Lab Waste Handling & Maintenance Guidelines v1

This document contains general instruction on how to handle the lab waste mostly from the cell culture room. The tasks described here are usually taken care of by technical staff (eg. lab manager). These guidelines, however, are meant to help researchers in case they need to take care of any of these tasks whenever there is no technical staff available (eg. sick leave, vacations, etc)

1. Biohazard Waste Bags

Transparent biohazard waste bags are in the main lab. Between the window and the bench on the left side (same side as the refrigerators)

Always ensure there are two bags installed in the biohazard container. In the Färkkilä lab, we usually have a yellow bag that is never removed, so this one counts as one already.

If bags run out, buy new ones from CityLab.

When a Biohazard Bag Is Nearly Full

Close the bag securely using red tape.

Attach a waste QR code to the bag.

QR codes are stored in a pipette case on top of the +4°C fridge in the cell culture room.

Bring the sealed bag to the waste container on the P floor.

Access requires a keycard.

Use the black door at the end of the room.

Dispose of the bag in the red zone.

Replacing the Bag

Install a new transparent biohazard bag immediately after disposal and secure it with tape so that it does not hang loose.

2. Pasteur Pipette Waste

When the Container Is Nearly Full

Add approximately:

4 L of water

8 Virkon tablets

Leave the container to disinfect for 3–8 days. Minimum required time: 3 days

After Disinfection

Pour the liquid down the sink.

Bring the disinfected pipettes to the glass waste container on the P floor. Do not discard the plastic container. We re-use them.

Return the empty container to the lab and rinse it with water 3 times.

Spare Containers

Empty replacement containers are stored below the cell culture hoods.

3. Cell Culture Liquid Waste (From Vacuum Pump)

General Information

Three vacuum bottles are shared between the Vaharautio and Färkkilä labs.

Only one spare bottle is available for both labs.

Monitor bottle levels carefully and replace them promptly when needed.

Locations

Spare bottle: below one of the cell culture hoods

Vacuum attachments: inside a pipette case on Anastasia’s bench in the main lab

Disinfectant (P3-Topax 990): on the floor between Anastasia’s bench and the fridge/freezer.

Replacing the Bottle

Add a small amount of disinfectant (P3-Topax 990) to the new bottle. Only enough to cover the bottom of the bottle is enough.

Screw clean vacuum attachments into the bottle.

Attach the new bottle to the vacuum system.

Disinfecting the Old Bottle

Add 6–8 Virkon tablets.

Cover the opening with foil.

Store the bottle in the sink in the cell culture room for 3–8 days. Minimum required time: 3 days

After disinfection, empty the liquid into the sink and bring the bottle for autoclaving (see Autoclaving section)

4. CO₂ Monitoring

Monitor the CO₂ levels regularly.

Once the marked limit is reached, call the number provided in the onboarding document. If Asked About CO₂ Bottles, inform them that the lab uses the bottles stored on the second floor.

5. Cardboard & Paper Waste

Disposal Procedure

Bring cardboard waste to the green recycling container on the lab floor. Keycard access is required.

The recycling container is located just before the dry ice containers.

6. Autoclaving

Preparing Materials

Place the materials to be autoclaved into the appropriate containers. Please, do this in Anastasia’s bench:

Pasteur pipettes in metal cases.

5 mL Eppendorf tubes in empty pipette cases.

Smaller Eppendorf tubes (eg. 2 mL, 1.5 mL, and PCR tubes) in autoclave bags from the autoclave room.

Then take them to the autoclave room.

Put autoclave tape to close the container and in another location.

Use only the tape provided in the autoclave room, not our own.

Label the autoclave tape with:

Date

Your initials

If “Färkkilä, 3 Krs” is not already written on the container, add it.
